questions.question_text: A Michigan seller lists her home with Broker A under an exclusive right to sell agreement. She then finds a buyer herself without the broker's help. What happens?
questions.correct_answer: Broker A still earns the commission
distractors: The seller pays only a flat fee
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive right to sell listing, the broker earns the commission regardless of who procures the buyer, even if the seller finds the buyer independently.

questions.question_text: Which type of listing agreement allows the seller to avoid paying a commission if the seller personally finds the buyer?
questions.explanation: An exclusive agency listing gives the broker exclusive marketing rights, but the seller retains the right to sell the property independently without owing a commission. An open listing also allows this but is non-exclusive.

questions.question_text: A Michigan broker presents an oral listing agreement to a seller. Is this enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: No, listing agreements must be written
distractors: Yes, if both parties agree verbally
Yes, if witnessed by a third party
Only if the property is under $100,000
questions.explanation: Michigan law requires all listing agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. An oral listing agreement does not meet this requirement.

questions.question_text: A seller signs an open listing with three different brokers. Broker B procures the buyer. Who earns the commission?
questions.explanation: Under an open listing, only the broker who actually procures the buyer earns the commission. Multiple brokers can hold open listings simultaneously, but only the procuring cause is paid.

questions.question_text: A Michigan listing agreement does not include a termination date. What is the status of this agreement?
distractors: It defaults to a 6-month statutory term
It defaults to a 12-month statutory term
It automatically renews month to month
questions.explanation: Michigan Admin Rule R 339.22131(2) requires every service provision agreement, including a listing agreement, to contain a definite expiration date. Using one without an expiration date is a licensing-rule violation that exposes the licensee to sanctions under the Occupational Code, such as fines or license suspension, but no statute or rule automatically voids the contract itself. Michigan has no statutory default term.

questions.question_text: Under the NAR settlement effective August 2024, when must a Michigan buyer sign a buyer broker agreement?
questions.explanation: The NAR settlement (August 2024) requires buyers to sign a written buyer broker agreement before the agent can begin showing or touring properties with the buyer.

questions.question_text: A buyer agent at a Michigan Realtor-affiliated brokerage drafts a buyer broker agreement but omits any mention of compensation. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: No, compensation amount or rate is required
distractors: Yes, compensation is always negotiated later
Yes, if the seller will pay the commission
Only if the buyer waives disclosure
questions.explanation: Under the NAR settlement rules effective August 2024, written buyer agreements must state the compensation amount or rate in a specific, objectively ascertainable way — it cannot be open-ended. Michigan's own rules separately require service provision agreements to be fully completed with a definite expiration date, but the compensation-disclosure mandate comes from the settlement.

questions.question_text: Under the NAR settlement rules, what statement must a buyer broker agreement include about commission rates?
questions.correct_answer: Fees are negotiable and not set by law
distractors: Fees are set by local MLS rules
Fees are determined by the seller
questions.explanation: The NAR settlement (August 2024) requires written buyer agreements to disclose conspicuously that broker fees and commissions are fully negotiable and not set by law. This is an MLS policy requirement followed by Michigan Realtor brokerages rather than a provision of Michigan's license law.

questions.question_text: A Michigan buyer broker agreement has no expiration date. What is the effect?
questions.correct_answer: The licensee has violated Michigan's licensing rules
questions.explanation: A buyer agency agreement is a service provision agreement under Michigan law, and Admin Rule R 339.22131(2) requires it to include a definite expiration date. Omitting the date subjects the licensee to discipline, such as fines or license suspension, under the Occupational Code, but it does not automatically invalidate the agreement. Michigan sets no default term or renewal period.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants to tour an open house with a new agent but has not signed a buyer broker agreement. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Have the buyer sign an agreement first
distractors: Proceed and sign after the tour
Only sign if the buyer makes an offer
No agreement is needed for open houses
questions.explanation: Under the NAR settlement rules effective August 2024, a written buyer broker agreement must be signed before any property touring, including open houses where the buyer's agent accompanies them.

questions.question_text: A Michigan salesperson works as an independent contractor under Broker X. Who supervises the salesperson's real estate activities?
distractors: No one, since the agent is an IC
questions.explanation: In Michigan, the sponsoring broker must supervise a salesperson's real estate activities regardless of whether the salesperson is classified as an employee or independent contractor for tax purposes.

questions.question_text: A Michigan salesperson independently closes a deal but makes a material misrepresentation within her duties. Who bears liability?
questions.correct_answer: Both the salesperson and sponsoring broker
distractors: Only the salesperson is held liable
Only the sponsoring broker is held liable
Neither, if the error was unintentional
questions.explanation: The sponsoring broker is liable for the actions of salespersons performed within the scope of their real estate activities. Both the salesperson and broker can face disciplinary action and liability.

questions.question_text: Under Michigan law, a salesperson MUST work under which arrangement?
questions.correct_answer: A sponsoring broker at all times
distractors: Any licensed agent as a mentor
questions.explanation: Michigan requires every salesperson to be sponsored by and work under a licensed broker. A salesperson cannot conduct real estate activities independently without broker sponsorship.

questions.question_text: A Michigan broker classifies her salesperson as an independent contractor for tax purposes. How does this affect the broker's supervisory duties?
questions.correct_answer: It does not reduce supervisory duties
distractors: It limits supervision to financial matters
It transfers supervision to the state
questions.explanation: Whether a salesperson is classified as an employee or independent contractor for tax purposes has no bearing on the broker's obligation to supervise real estate activities. Supervision is required regardless.

questions.question_text: In Michigan, which employment classifications are available for a salesperson working under a broker?
questions.explanation: Michigan salespersons may be classified as either employees or independent contractors under their sponsoring broker. The classification affects tax treatment but not the broker's supervisory responsibilities.
